---
title: "Gene set-focused analysis of RNA-seq data with MIEP"
author: "Alberto Corradin, and Vincenzo Ciminale"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%m/%d/%Y')`"
abstract: >
  *Make-it-easy-pipeline* (MIEP) is an integrated, interactive, and user-friendly 
  R package that aggregates a high number of tools for the analysis of RNA-seq 
  data. A modern approach to biological targets identification is implemented in 
  MIEP. Since pathway disruption plays a critical role in cancer development by affecting 
  the signaling mechanisms that rule cell function, focusing on gene sets mimicking 
  pathways can accelerate the development and testing of treatments acting 
  on key elements of signal transduction. MIEP pipeline develops and edits new gene 
  sets based on the results of multiple integrated analyses.
output:
  rmarkdown::html_document:
    highlight: pygments
    toc: true
    fig_width: 5
bibliography: library.bib
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Gene set-focused analysis of RNA-seq data with MIEP pipeline}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
---

```{r setup, echo=FALSE, results="hide"}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(tidy = FALSE,
                      cache = FALSE,
                      dev = "png",
                      message = FALSE, error = FALSE, warning = TRUE)
```	



**Note:** if you use MIEP in published research, please cite:

> Corradin, A., Ciminale, V. (2024), 
> MIEP: *Make-it-easy-pipeline*,
> useR! 2024 conference, 8-12 July 2024, Salzburg, Austria



# Quick start

MIEP performs integrated analyses of RNA-seq data providing gene set-focused 
results by means of an interactive pipeline. 
MIEP's pipeline can be started by running the following user-friendly R command:  
> *MIEP()*.  
This command assumes you can provide the paths required as inputs in a text file
to select interactively. Alternatively, you can supply the same information 
directly in the R shell by passing required paths as parameters:   
> *MIEP(filePath1=".../projectName/pipeline", filePath2=".../myDatabase/gmtFiles")*.

Parameter *"filePath1"* represents the path to the folder where MIEP will store 
the results of the pipeline. In details, the user is expected to choose the name 
of the research project and to create corresponding directory (*".../projectName"*). 
Then he/she shall create subfolder *"pipeline"* (i.e. *".../projectName/pipeline"*) 
where MIEP's pipeline will insert the results deriving from its integrated analyses.   
Similarly, data to analyze shall be provided by the user in a subfolder having 
the following path: *".../projectName/pipeline/data"*. The user is charged 
to create and fill it with the required data files, which will be described in 
the following paragraphs.

Parameter *"filePath2"* is optional. It represents the path to the folder containing 
the Gene Matrix Transposed files (\*.gmt) that are of interest for the user. These 
latter can be downloaded from GSEA [@Subramanian2005] web site 
(www.gsea-msigdb.org/gsea/index.jsp).
If parameter is missing (namely *filePath2=NULL*), MIEP will proceed by considering 
only the gene sets generated in consequence of the pipeline's integrated analyses. 


```{r quickStart, eval=FALSE}
# fast start
MIEP() # it follows the uploading of a text file providing required file paths
# full start
MIEP(filePath1=".../projectName/pipeline",filePath2=".../myDatabase/gmtFiles")
# paths are passed as parameters
```

# How to get help for MIEP

Any user can write directly to the creator of MIEP (*Make-it-easy-pipeline*)
at *alberto.corradin@iov.veneto.it*.

# Acknowledgments

Dr. Donna Mia D'Agostino,
Dr. Francesco Ciccarese,
Dr. Vittoria Raimondi,
Dr. Loredana Urso,
Dr. Micol Silic-Benussi.

# Funding

MIEP and its developers have been partially supported by funding from AIRC IG#24935


# Input data files

The first step of MIEP's pipeline consists in performing statistical tests by 
*DESeq2* [@Love2014]. To this aim, raw gene counts shall be provided together with
a file describing samples involved in the performed experiment. In details, 
the tab-separated-value file *"raw_gene_counts.tsv"* and the comma-separated-value 
file *"coldata.csv"* shall be inserted in data folder, where they will searched
by the scripts composing MIEP's pipeline. 

MIEP demands the user to specifically indicate in a text file the control samples 
for statistical testing. This customisable approach allows to compare both treated 
samples against untreated ones, and distinct treatments between them. 
To facilitate the export and transmission of the normalized gene counts data set, 
an *ExpressionSet* R object is going to be developed by MIEP in the course of the 
pipeline. However, its metadata, namely author, laboratory, contacts, and title 
of the experiment, should be supplied by the user in a tab-separated-values file.

Examples of mentioned input files, included *"controls.txt"* and  *"infoExprSet.tsv"*, 
are provided in folder *".../MIEP/inst/extdata"* of MIEP source package. 

Without any of the above described data files, pipeline cannot fully develop its 
potentialities. Controlled exits are planned innconsequence of exception handling, 
followed by friendly indications for the user. These latter will suggest the 
missing file. 

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 1: Extract of an *in silico* data set in the format expected by DESeq2 for the *"raw_genes_counts.tsv"* file'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/rawGeneCounts.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 2: Corresponding *"coldata.csv"* file. This is in comma-separated-value format, i.e. values in a row are separated by comma instead of tab '}
knitr::include_graphics('images/coldata.png')
```

# MIEP wraps already available algorithms

MIEP R package aggregates a high number of tools for the analysis of RNA-seq data, 
and facilitates their exploitation by wrapping algorithms and supporting results 
by *ad hoc* graphics. Summarizing, MIEP can help biologists thanks to wrappers 
that provide a careful handling of exceptions, helping the user to understand 
potential lacks or faults in imputed data. Most important, MIEP provides an 
extended graphical support where results are summarized, granting their immediate 
understanding.

In detials, results of each tool included in MIEp's pipeline are reported in tables 
in HTML format or by pictures (heat maps, volcano plots,...). Taken together, these 
characteristics make MIEP an easy-to-use tool for biologists having only a basic 
knowledge of programming. The following paragraphs will detail algorithms composing 
the pipeline and their wrappers.

# Standard workflow
## Pre-filtering low count genes before running *DESeq2* functions

*DESeq2* performs statistical tests, but pre-filtering low-count genes before 
running *DESeq2* functions is recommended by this package's authors. MIEP provides 
two filters called *"batch-filter"* and *"pheno-filter"*, respectively.

Batch filter acts on the total reads' copy number per row, i.e. per feature since 
every row in the input file *"raw_gene_counts.tsv"* reports the number of copies 
detected for a single sequence in the data set. By means of *batch-filter* 
the user can discard all the features showing an average copy number per sample 
lower than the imputed threshold. We suggest to apply *batch-filter* when some 
genes of particular interest are expected to appear only in a subset of samples, 
being barely absent elsewhere instead. Default threshold is set to zero (no *batch-filter*) 
because the above described situation represents a particular case. In other words,
settings shall be changed by the user to perform this filtering.

Second filter, *pheno-filter*, considers reads' counts in every couple "cell line-
condition". In place of cell lines, an experiment could involve multiple 
patients, alternatively. On the other side, term "conditions" is used to indicate 
different treatments or time instants in a time course under observation. 
Let's consider the most diffused design: multiple cell lines and multiple 
treatments, for the sake of simplicity. *Pheno-filter* keeps only the features 
that show copy numbers higher than the imputed threshold in the majority of samples 
composing every couple "cell line-condition". In other words, a sequence is removed 
when it is barely absent in at least one couple "cell line-condition".

*Shiny* apps [@Chang2023] allow the user changing MIEP's default settings at run 
time. Results of filtering are stored in the subfolder: 
*".../pipeline/(results folder)/DESeq2runs*, where the directory "results folder" 
is automatically created by MIEP's pipeline to store the ensemble of results. 
Results folder will be named based on the date the pipeline has been run. 

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 3: *Shiny* apps to change DESeq2 default settings'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/settings.png')
```

## Statistical tests when data are biased

The presence of biases due to cell line (or patient) specificity (or batch effect) 
is verified in advance by MIEP R package thanks to Principal Component Analysis (PCA). 
These preliminary PCA plots are generated and stored in the following subfolder: 
*".../DESeq2runs/wholeBatch/basicImages"*, together with some more images 
reporting initial analyses, referring to the whole data set. 

Let's consider the most diffused design again (multiple cell lines and multiple 
treatments). In case of mentioned biases, e.g. cell line specificity that overwhelms 
the effects of treatments (as shown in Figure 4), MIEP will compare conditions in 
the subset of samples referring to the same cell line by Wald test (single-factor 
approach). In other words, statistical test is performed for every cell line 
separately, by comparing conditions.
Consequently, MIEP will create one folder per cell line, and corresponding
subfolders named as follows *".../DESeq2runs/test-(cell line)/testTables"*, 
where the results of statistical tests are reported. If patients are studied 
instead of cell line, the subfolder *".../DESeq2runs/test-(patient ID)"* will 
appear instead.


```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 4: Specifity of patients overwhelms the effect of treatment'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/ggPCAplot_ntd.png')
```

## Data shrinkage

Users can choose among three methods of data shrinkage to approach potential 
outliers: *apeglm* [@Zhu2018], *ashr* [@Stephens2023], and normal. By default MIEP 
does not apply anyone of them but test their effects on data. This preliminary 
comparison shown by *MA* plots is stored in the sub folder 
*".../test-(cell line)/graphicInspection/(name of the comparison)/beforeShrinkage"*. 
Front to graphics, the user can select the shrinkage method to apply and re-run the 
pipeline based on his/her preference by changing default settings at run time. 


```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 5: Comparison of shrinkage methods'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/shrinkageMethodsComparison.png')
```


## Differentially expressed features

Biologists are usually interested in differentially expressed sequences. 
These are usually interpreted as a subset of statistically significant features 
to selected based on fold change values. Linear fold change of 2 is MIEP's default 
because the application of such a threshold is quiet common in the literature. 
However, an alternative is proposed in MIEP to address landscapes that differ from 
the usual. For example, at early time points in time series, gene expression 
development is still in progress. In such a situation, the user could prefer a 
threshold lower than 2. To avoid arbitrary choices, MIEP's pipeline enables the
calculation of the distribution of fold change values. Then, it sets the threshold 
to select differentially expressed features in correspondence of the 95th percentile. 
In other words, only statistically significant sequences that show fold changes 
belonging to top 5% of values are kept. Calculation is repeated ofor every comparison.
Volcano plots showing the established thresholds are stored in subfolders: 
*".../graphicInspection/(name of the comparison)/afterShrinkage"*, 
whereas details concerning threshold's calculation are reported in text files.

Once differentially expressed features have been selected, an *ad hoc* file that 
reports test's results for this subset of sequences only is added in folder 
*".../DESeq2runs/test-(cell line)/testTables"*.

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 6: Default thresholds for the selection of differentially expressed features are shown in Volcano plot'}
knitr::include_graphics('./images/volcanoPlotWithBoundaries.png')
```

## Annotations

Following annotation of sequences on the basis of Hugo Gene nomenclature (HGCN, 
https://www.genenames.org/), the following categories are discarded: 
long intergenic non-protein coding RNA (lncRNA), uncharacterized open reading 
frames, families with sequence similarity, small nucleolar RNA, 
pseudogenes, divergent transcripts, novel proteins, intronic transcripts, 
competing endogenous lncRNA, overlapping transcripts, since this first version of 
MIEP's pipeline was developed to investigate coding genes. 

For next-generation-sequencing (NGS) procedures targeted to detect microRNAs, 
corresponding sequences can be kept following a user's specification. In 
other words, by changing default settings (see *Shiny* app in Figure 7).

The data set resulting from annotations is stored in the folder: 
*".../pipeline/(results folder)/normalizedCounts"* 

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 7: *Shiny* app to change default settings when filtering features based on their annotations'}
knitr::include_graphics('./images/microRNAs.png')
```

## Features selection

Genes that resulted differentially expressed in at least one of the comparisons
among conditions (e.g. "treatment vs control"), are collected to compose distinct 
subsets characterizing the behavior of each cell line (or patient). These subsets 
are analyzed separately by the other tools included in MIEP's pipeline. 
The union of differentially expressed genes is also reported to be subject to 
the following classification based on machine learning (see paragraph "Conditional 
random forests").
The data sets of differentially expressed genes are stored in subfolders named: 
*".../(results folder)/DEs_(cell line)/featuresSelection/"*. 

Heat maps are generated from every subset of differentially expressed features, 
at first without any correction of potential biases. The hierarchical clustering 
shown below highlights the predominance of patient-specific traits, confirming PCA 
in Figure 4. 
After having Z-score transformed data, samples appear clustered by treatment instead. 
This suggests that Z-score transformation can enhance functional traits and 
reduce the effects of biases due to patients (or cell line) specificity.

Heat maps are stored in folders: *".../featuresSelection/heatmaps/beforeBiasCorrection"*
and *".../heatmaps/afterBiasCorrection"*, respectively.



```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Figure 8: Hierarchical clustering of based on reads' counts. Patients' specificity overwhelms the effects of treatment as previously indicated by PCA. Custom palette specifically designed to highlight differences in gene expression."}
knitr::include_graphics('./images/heatmap_before.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 9: Hierarchical clustering of based on Z-score transformed data. Effects of bias were clearly reduced, making data comparable. Custom palette specifically designed to highlight differences in gene expression.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/heatmap_after.png')
```

## Dimentionality reduction

Results of Z-score transformation are further investigated by means of PCA 
[@Kassambara2020], confirming its effectiveness: samples are mainly separated 
based on the treatment (see Figure 10). 

Interestingly, PCA allows ranking genes based on the weights (*importance*) they 
participate to the composition of principal components with. Figure 11 shows genes
having the highest *importance* values for first principal component. MIEP allows 
highlighting in the picture the features that belong to a particular gene set of 
interest. This last can be indicated by the user at run time by means of specific 
*Shiny* app. Additional indications about reads' counts can suggest biologists 
preferable targets for wet lab experiments.

Dimensionality reduction is carried out also by means of UMAP [@Melville2023]; 
both 2D and 3D representations are supplied. Mapped data are visualized also by 
exploiting tSNE [@Krijthe2015] and SVM [@Meyer2023]. All the results and graphical 
representations derived from dimensionality reduction are stored in a folder named 
*".../DEs_(cell line)/dimensionalityReduction"*

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 10: PCA on Z-score trasformed data confirms correction of the bias due to specificity of patients.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/PCA3D.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Figure 11: Genes' *importance*, i.e. their weights when composing first principal component."}
knitr::include_graphics('images/PC1_50topContributors.png')
```

## Enrichment of Gene Ontology terms

The enrichment of Gene Ontology (GO) terms can be verified thanks to *topGO* 
R package [@Alexa2023]. The algorithm allows testing GO terms enrichment on the 
basis of a subset of genes entitled as *"significant"* to distinguish them from 
remaining features composing the *"gene universe"*. Differentially expressed genes 
are an obvious choice as *"significant"* subset. 

However, an alternative subset of *"significant"* genes can derive from the 
ranking of *importance* in composing principal components, e.g. by selecting the 
top 5% subset based on the weights established by PCA. In fact, when samples are 
separated based on treatments, the genes composing principal components with the 
highest weights are likely involved in the functional mechanisms of interest. 

Results of GO terms enrichment's tests are reported in details in subfolders 
named: *".../DEs_(cell line)/GOenrichment_basedOn(name of significant set)"*.
Once identified the most enriched GO terms, corresponding gene sets will be 
automatically edited by MIEP, based on the nodes graphs generated by *topGO* R 
package.  The Gene Matrix Transposed files (\*.gmt) files created by MIEP's 
pipeline are automatically copied in folder *".../pipeline/(results folder)/gmtFiles"* ,
easily accessible to the user.


```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 12: *Shiny* app to select the subset of genes to consider as *significant* with respect to the *gene universe*. User can choose among differentially expressed features, or on the basis of PCA results, or submit custom lists to the algorithm that will calculate the enrichment of gene ontology terms.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/geneOntology.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 13: An example of ranking of GO terms.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/rankingOfGOterms.png')
```

## Conditional random forests

Conditional random forests [@Hothorn2006;@Zeileis2008;@HothornT2006] are 
used to classify samples depending on condition (e.g. different treatments or 
distinct time points). Potentially, also depending on cell line (or patient). 
*Variables importance* of features is then calculated [@Strobl2007;@Strobl2008]. 
Genes characterized by outstanding *importance* values are used to edit new gene 
sets, likely related conditions. Results are stored in folder:
*".../(results folder)/DEs_union/conditionalRandomForests/"* .

The settings of random forests' parameters are optimized by means of a grid search 
approach [@Gilli2019;@Schumann2023], whose computational burden is eased by 
parallel computing. Distinct implementations of parallel computing [@Weston2022;@Solymos2023] 
are automatically tested before grid search, to select the fastest. 
Since expanded swap memory is often used as surrogate when RAM is limited, 
swappiness in Linux OS is automatically increased for machine learning by invoking 
system commands. A *Shiny* app allows inserting superuser password for the execution 
of system calls. 


```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 14: *Shiny* app to select what classes to classify for.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/shinyForRandomForests.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Figure 15: Features' importance represents their usefulness to separated treated samples from controls (classification by condition)."}
knitr::include_graphics('images/varImportance.png')
```

## Focusing on single gene sets

In conclusion, MIEP R package helps developing testable hypotheses and selecting 
targets for wet lab functional testing by editing gene new gene sets based on the 
results of the above described analyses. Figure 16 shows the heat map of top 
features, as selected based on random forests' classification. Corresponding
fold changes are graphically reported in Figure 17. 

Analogous images are automatically produced by MIEP for gene sets derived from 
the enrichment of GO terms. These final pictures, generated for every gene set of 
interest, can provide indications of immediate understanding for biologists planning 
subsequent wet lab tests. Results are stored in subfolders: 
*".../(results folder)/genelistsOverview/(gene set)"* .


```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 16: Heat map of the features showing the highest *variables importance* in classification.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/heatmapDEonly_customColors.png')
```

```{r, out.width='75%', echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap='Figure 17: Fold changes of genes in Figure 16 are reported graphically.'}
knitr::include_graphics('images/heatmapFC_customColors.png')
```
